findings.description: Full PAN (card_number) and CVV are written to the application log in cleartext at INFO level, a direct PCI-DSS violation (CVV must never be persisted post-auth; PAN must be masked) and CWE-532 sensitive-data-in-log exposure.
The logging call's argument list passes raw cvv and card_number into the log record, persisting cardholder data to any log sink/aggregator that ingests this logger (CWE-312/CWE-532).

refund() acts on any caller-supplied order_id with no ownership/authorization check, allowing an attacker to refund (and via state change, disrupt) arbitrary paid orders — Broken Access Control / IDOR (CWE-862, OWASP A01).

charge_order() exposes no authorization/ownership check on order_id and accepts caller-supplied card data, enabling charging against arbitrary orders and updating their payment state (IDOR / Missing Authorization, CWE-862, OWASP A01).

monthly_revenue_report() returns aggregate financial/revenue data with no access-control gate on the entry point, risking sensitive business-data disclosure if reachable by an unauthorized caller (CWE-862, OWASP A01).

Transaction IDs are derived from Python's non-cryptographic, low-entropy hash of (last-4-of-card, amount), making them predictable and collision-prone as financial identifiers (CWE-340 / CWE-330); acceptable only because this is an elided SDK stub.

discount_pct (sourced from the DB order row) is applied with no bounds validation, so a negative or >100 value alters the charged amount — a price-manipulation/fraud vector if the discount field is attacker-influenceable.

If gateway.charge() succeeds but the subsequent UPDATE/commit raises, the customer is charged while the order stays unpaid, risking double-charge on retry (financial-integrity / atomicity gap; defer detail to logic critic).

Monetary amounts use float (unit_price, totals, tax), introducing rounding error in charged/reported amounts; prefer integer cents or Decimal (correctness with a fraud-rounding edge; defer to logic critic).
